narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in some positive ways, but the admissions model focused on key indicators from past students' paths to the bar exam. The model predicts a 73.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The decision hinged on just a few standout factors from your record, ranked by their influence on this outcome. Most critically, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the typical student's 5.53—strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar failure, as it reflects weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next, your LSAT score of 26, well under the average of 36.70, further pushed the prediction toward failure by highlighting a gap in the standardized skills that often predict bar success. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, close to the dataset average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor, nudging the model slightly toward expecting you to pass. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles by Year 3 and score much stronger on the LSAT, your academics in these areas placed you among those historically more likely to struggle. Your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time status aligned with the norm and didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from your background—many in similar situations have succeeded with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and seeking bar prep resources early. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view dramatically, opening doors at schools that value persistence. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
